metadata.description: E2E real application closed-loop validation on Delta

parsed.diagnosis: This job exhibits severe I/O inefficiency due to small transfer sizes and excessive system call overhead. With 91,505 POSIX writes totaling 1.4GB, the average write size is only ~15KB, far below the optimal 1MB+ threshold. The job has 137,694 unaligned file accesses and achieves only 16.8 MB/s total bandwidth despite good sequential access patterns (94.2% sequential writes). The high write count combined with small transfer sizes creates syscall overhead that dominates I/O time.

recommendations.explanation: The job performs 91,505 small writes averaging 15KB each, causing excessive syscall overhead. Each small write incurs kernel transition costs that dominate actual data transfer time.
recommendations.code_before: for (i=0; i<n; i++) write(fd, buf, 15470);  /* 15KB per call */
recommendations.expected_speedup: 2-4x based on KB evidence from similar small write patterns
recommendations.api_change: POSIX write buffering -> large chunk writes

recommendations.explanation: Despite good sequential access (94.2%), throughput is underutilized at 16.8 MB/s due to small transfer sizes and potential single-OST contention. The 768 fsyncs also add synchronization overhead.
recommendations.code_before:
write(fd, buf, small_size);
fsync(fd);  /* Frequent sync calls */
recommendations.expected_speedup: 1.5-2x based on reduced sync overhead and larger transfers
recommendations.api_change: Frequent small writes+sync -> batched large writes

recommendations.explanation: 137,694 unaligned file accesses indicate data is not properly aligned to filesystem block boundaries, reducing I/O efficiency and potentially causing read-modify-write cycles.
recommendations.expected_speedup: 1.2-1.5x based on elimination of read-modify-write cycles
recommendations.api_change: Unaligned POSIX writes -> aligned buffer writes

parsed.overall_expected_improvement: 3-8x total speedup (multiplicative effects of buffering + alignment + reduced sync)

parsed.diagnosis: This job exhibits poor I/O throughput utilization (44.25 MB/s) despite highly sequential writes (99.77% sequential). The primary issues are: (1) Using POSIX I/O for shared-file parallel writes across 64 ranks, causing serialization and coordination overhead, and (2) Excessive synchronization with 128 fsync calls for only 1314 writes (1 fsync per ~10 writes), forcing frequent disk flushes that block subsequent I/O operations.

recommendations.explanation: The job uses POSIX write() calls for shared-file parallel I/O across 64 ranks. Each rank independently seeks and writes, causing serialization at the file system level and missing opportunities for collective optimization.
Excessive fsync frequency (128 fsyncs for 1314 writes = ~1 fsync per 10 writes) forces premature disk flushes, blocking subsequent I/O and reducing pipeline efficiency.

parsed.overall_expected_improvement: 3-8x speedup (combining MPI-IO collective benefits with reduced synchronization overhead)

parsed.diagnosis: This job exhibits poor I/O throughput utilization (11.27 MB/s) despite sequential write patterns (99.56% sequential). The primary issues are: (1) Excessive synchronization with 128 fsyncs across only 675 writes (19% fsync rate), causing frequent disk flushes that serialize I/O operations, and (2) Use of POSIX I/O for shared-file parallel writes with 64 processes, which lacks coordination and likely causes contention. The large stride pattern (66MB) suggests processes are writing to different file regions but without proper collective coordination.

recommendations.explanation: Job performs 128 fsyncs for only 675 writes (19% fsync rate), causing excessive synchronization that serializes I/O operations and prevents parallel throughput scaling
recommendations.expected_speedup: 2-5x based on removing excessive synchronization overhead
recommendations.api_change: Remove per-write fsync calls, keep only final fsync

recommendations.explanation: Using POSIX I/O for shared-file parallel writes with 64 processes causes coordination overhead and potential contention, as each rank independently seeks and writes without collective optimization
recommendations.expected_speedup: 1.5-3x from collective I/O coordination and reduced contention

parsed.overall_expected_improvement: 3-15x total speedup (multiplicative effect of removing excessive fsync + collective I/O coordination)
